metadata.summary: Position first, then post 3-5x daily and reply 20-30x daily to large accounts. Threads and build-in-public posts drive growth. Hook is the first 15 words. Links reduce reach; put them in replies.

content.title: Twitter/X Growth
content.summary: A positioning-first Twitter/X growth system: define a unique angle, post 3-5 times daily, write 1-2 threads per week, and reply 20-30 times daily to accounts larger than you. The first 15 words of every tweet are the hook. Thread tweet 1 must standalone; external links in tweets reduce reach — put them in the first reply or pinned tweet instead.

why.p: Twitter/X rewards consistency, personality, and value. Build-in-public posts with real numbers (MRR, user count, specific percentages) outperform vague narrative posts 2-5x on engagement. Being a consistent early voice in 3-5 large accounts' comments builds recognition faster than standalone posting because it borrows existing audience attention. X API Basic tier limits post creation to 1,500 posts/month — track usage to avoid exhausting quota.

list.item: Generating weekly tweet batches (15-20 drafts) and thread outlines across content pillars
Drafting bio, pinned tweet, and banner copy for profile optimization
Producing build-in-public update templates (monthly revenue, wins/losses, next steps)
Analyzing Twitter Analytics CSV export to identify top hooks and best posting times
Writing a thread: hook tweet + promise tweet + 5-8 value tweets + summary + CTA
Automated posting or replying — bot-style automation risks account suspension
Generating content for trending topics without current context — agent's trend knowledge is outdated
Mass DM outreach — X ToS prohibits unsolicited commercial DMs at scale
Engagement pods — coordinated inauthentic engagement violates platform rules
New accounts (under 1K followers) using engagement metrics calibrated for established accounts

rule.statement: Define your Twitter identity across four elements before posting: topic (what you talk about), angle (what is unique), voice (how you sound), and format (content types).
rule.rationale: Without a clear positioning, followers cannot predict what they will get and have no reason to follow. Positioning creates expectation and repeat engagement.

rule.statement: Bio formula: [What you do] | Building [product] | [Social proof] | [CTA]. Keep under 160 characters.
rule.rationale: The bio is the first conversion surface. Visitors who land on your profile from a reply or mention will decide in 3 seconds whether to follow based on the bio.

rule.statement: Pinned tweet should be the best-performing content or a clear offer. Update it when a new high-performer emerges.
rule.rationale: The pinned tweet is the second thing visitors see after the bio. It should demonstrate content quality and provide a CTA.

section.name: thread-structure

rule.statement: Thread tweet 1 must standalone as a compelling post without reading the rest — it is the main distribution surface. External links reduce reach; put links in the first reply or bio.
rule.rationale: X's algorithm uses tweet 1 engagement (likes, retweets, bookmarks) to decide whether to distribute the rest of the thread. A weak tweet 1 kills the whole thread.

list.item: Tweet 1: Hook — problem or bold claim
Tweet 2: Promise — what they will learn
Tweets 3-8: Value — the actual content
Tweet 9: Summary + CTA
Tweet 10: Follow ask

example.title: Thread hook for build-in-public account
example.note: I went from $0 to $15K MRR in 8 months without paid ads. Here's the exact playbook: [thread emoji] — specific numbers, promise of value, clear format signal.

antipattern.description: Thread hook: "I have 10 tips for growing on Twitter [thread]"
antipattern.reason: Generic number-list hooks are overused. The algorithm sees low engagement on tweet 1 and does not distribute the thread. Use niche-specific, counterintuitive hooks instead.

section.reference: 10-tweet thread structure with hook, promise, value, summary, and CTA slots
Agent prompt for 15 tweets + 1 thread + 1 build-in-public update per week

section.name: reply-strategy

rule.statement: Reply to 20-30 accounts larger than you daily using the [React] + [Add value] + [Question or take] formula. Be first or early with a valuable reply.
rule.rationale: Being a consistent, early, thoughtful voice in 3-5 large accounts' comments builds recognition faster than standalone posting because it borrows existing audience attention.

rule.statement: Build-in-public posts with real numbers (MRR, user count, specific percentages) outperform vague narrative posts by 2-5x on engagement.
rule.rationale: Specificity signals authenticity and provides concrete value. Audiences can benchmark their own progress against real numbers; they cannot benchmark against vague claims.

section.reference: Monthly revenue update: MRR, users, churn, wins, losses, next

rule.statement: Track profile visits, follower growth rate, engagement rate, thread views, and newsletter signups from Twitter as primary KPIs.
rule.rationale: Follower count alone is misleading. Profile visits and newsletter signups measure conversion from attention to audience relationship.

list.item: Profile visits: 500/week = good, 2K/week = great
Follower growth: 50/week = good, 200/week = great
Engagement rate: 2% = good, 5%+ = great
Thread views: 5K = good, 50K+ = great

rule.statement: Follow → Newsletter → Free users → Paid customers. Twitter converts best to email; email converts to customers. Do not try to sell directly from tweets.
rule.rationale: Twitter followers are an attention asset, not a customer list. Email captures that attention in a higher-intent, lower-distraction channel where conversion rates are 5-10x higher.

list.item: Pin a tweet with your best offer
CTA in bio pointing to newsletter or product
Build in public — show product progress and customer wins
Limited-time offers for followers in threads

antipattern.description: Inconsistent posting — missing days or weeks.
antipattern.reason: X's algorithm reduces distribution for accounts with erratic cadence. Even one tweet per day maintains algorithm favor; missing multiple days resets momentum.

antipattern.description: Only promotional content — no value posts.
antipattern.reason: Promotional-only accounts get unfollowed. The 80% value / 20% promo ratio is minimum; accounts under 1K followers should be 95/5.
X significantly reduces distribution of tweets with external links. Links belong in the first reply, pinned tweet, or bio.

section.name: agent-and-api-constraints

rule.statement: X API Basic tier allows 1,500 posts/month. Track usage if using agent-driven scheduling — bulk generation can exhaust quota and lock out manual posting.
rule.rationale: Quota exhaustion blocks all automated and manual API-authenticated actions. Preserve quota for the highest-value posting windows.

section.reference: Parse Twitter Analytics CSV export to compute engagement rate and identify top hook patterns

rule.statement: The first 15 words of every tweet are the hook — if they do not create curiosity or promise value, nothing else matters.
rule.rationale: Readers make snap decisions in the first line; weak hooks receive no engagement regardless of content quality below.

rule.statement: Thread tweet 1 must standalone as a compelling post without reading the rest; it is the main distribution surface.
rule.rationale: X's algorithm uses tweet 1 engagement to decide whether to distribute the rest of the thread.

rule.statement: External links in tweets reduce reach significantly on X; put links in the first reply or in the bio/pinned tweet instead.
rule.rationale: The X algorithm suppresses posts with external links in the body, reducing organic reach substantially.

rule.statement: Build-in-public posts with real numbers (MRR, user count, specific percentage) outperform vague narrative posts by 2-5x on engagement.
rule.rationale: Specificity signals authenticity and provides concrete value that audiences can benchmark against.

rule.statement: Reply strategy compounds: being a consistent, early, thoughtful voice in 3-5 large accounts' comments builds recognition faster than standalone posting.
rule.rationale: Comments are seen by the original poster's entire audience and at the top of discussion threads; early valuable replies borrow existing attention.

rule.statement: Schedule posts but do not automate replies — algorithmic detection of reply bots has increased; manual engagement is detectable as human and preferred.
rule.rationale: Platform preference for authentic human engagement is a documented policy; bot-detected accounts lose distribution.

section.name: agent-integration-notes

rule.statement: Two-phase approach: (1) weekly content generation — Haiku call produces 20-30 tweet drafts and 2 thread outlines from pillar definitions and brand voice; (2) monthly performance review — Sonnet call analyzes analytics CSV.
rule.rationale: Separation of high-volume content generation from strategic analysis leverages each model's strengths and ensures human judgment on strategy.

rule.statement: Human selects, personalizes, and schedules all posts; agent handles drafting and analysis only. Human executes all posting and engagement.
rule.rationale: Agents cannot adapt to real-time context, trending conversations, or account-specific voice nuances; human curation is mandatory.

section.p: Tweet hooks that perform well are highly context-specific (niche, audience maturity, current events); agent hooks are structural templates, not guaranteed performers.
X algorithm behavior (distribution of threads vs single tweets, effect of external links) changes frequently and is undocumented; validate agent advice quarterly against native analytics.
Build-in-public content requires real data (MRR, user counts, churn); the agent can produce the template but cannot fill in authentic numbers.
The "reply to large accounts" strategy requires knowing which accounts are currently relevant in the niche — agent cannot browse X to identify them; human must curate the list.
Analytics CSV structure varies by export method (Twitter Analytics native, Tweetdeck, third-party); agent must be given column names to parse correctly.
If the agent includes links in tweet drafts, flag them for relocation to first comment before posting to preserve reach.
Agent thread hooks default to "I went from X to Y in Z days" — this pattern is overused; push for niche-specific, counterintuitive hooks.
Agent cannot see current trending conversations; strategy recommendations based on "join trending conversations" require human judgment on what is actually trending now.
Engagement advice calibrated for established accounts (5K+ followers) does not apply to new accounts; adjust expectations in prompts for early-stage accounts.
X platform rules and API access have changed rapidly since 2022; any agent recommendation about automation or API use should be validated against current ToS.

template.purpose: 10-tweet thread structure: hook, promise, value, summary, CTA
Monthly revenue update: MRR, users, churn, wins, losses, next
Agent prompt: 15 tweets + 1 thread + 1 BIP update
Parse Twitter Analytics CSV → engagement rate + top 5 hooks
